source.title: Bandai Namco Galaga Web history/game page

derivedAssertions.paraphrase: Galaga is an arcade space shooter released by Namco in 1981 and positioned as a successor to Galaxian.
derivedAssertions.conformanceUse: Use as high-authority identity grounding for Aurora's Galaga-like target, not as a pixel or timing target.

derivedAssertions.paraphrase: The player moves the fighter left and right and fires cannon shots, with only two shots active on screen.
derivedAssertions.conformanceUse: Supports shot-cap, horizontal-motion, and control simplicity checks.

derivedAssertions.paraphrase: At the start of each stage, enemy formations fly into the screen before settling into lines.
derivedAssertions.conformanceUse: Supports the requirement that aliens visibly arrive through authored paths rather than simply appearing in place.

derivedAssertions.paraphrase: The force is described in three role families: bosses, guards, and grunts.
derivedAssertions.conformanceUse: Maps to Aurora boss, butterfly/escort, and bee/line roles, while leaving exact sprite scoring to measured sprite artifacts.

derivedAssertions.paraphrase: As stages progress, enemies show varied flight patterns and special formation attacks.
derivedAssertions.conformanceUse: Supports strict penalties when later Aurora stages are only faster repetitions of earlier movement.

derivedAssertions.paraphrase: Boss Galaga can capture the player fighter; rescuing that fighter creates Dual Fighter mode with more firepower and a wider risk profile.
derivedAssertions.conformanceUse: Grounds capture, rescue, carried-fighter, and dual-fighter transition metrics.

derivedAssertions.paraphrase: Challenging stages are part of the game's long-term depth and replay enjoyment.
derivedAssertions.conformanceUse: Supports treating challenge stages as authored score exhibitions with novelty, not as filler waves.

measurementLimits: This source provides high-authority design grounding but does not provide enough frame-level data for trajectory or timing scores.
Use measured gameplay windows, contact sheets, and path labels for movement conformance.
Use reference sprite and audio artifacts for pixel and waveform conformance.
